text:
DEPARTMENT OF DIAGNOSTIC RADIOLOGY
NUCLEAR MEDICINE AND BODY CT REPORT

Patient: Raymond Kowalski
Date of Birth: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
Ordering Physician: [Urology / Oncology, name on file]
Indication: Newly diagnosed high-grade prostatic adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5), PSA 187.4 ng/mL. Staging evaluation for osseous and visceral metastatic disease.

------------------------------------------------------------
EXAMINATION 1: WHOLE BODY BONE SCINTIGRAPHY (Tc-99m MDP)
------------------------------------------------------------

TECHNIQUE:
Approximately 25.0 mCi of technetium-99m methylene diphosphonate (Tc-99m MDP) was injected intravenously. Whole-body planar images were acquired in anterior and posterior projections at approximately 3 hours post-injection using a dual-headed gamma camera. Supplemental SPECT/CT images were obtained through the lumbar spine and pelvis to provide anatomic correlation of equivocal findings. Delayed spot views were performed as needed.

FINDINGS:

Skull and Facial Bones:
No abnormal areas of increased or photopenic tracer activity within the calvarium, facial bones, or mandible. Symmetric, homogeneous uptake throughout.

Cervical and Thoracic Spine:
No focal abnormal tracer accumulation is identified within the cervical vertebrae (C1–C7) or thoracic vertebrae (T1–T12). Tracer distribution is symmetric and proportionate. No areas of segmental photopenia or focal hot spots.

Lumbar Spine:
Focal, intense tracer uptake is identified within the L3 vertebral body, involving the central and right paracentral portions of the body. A second focus of markedly increased tracer activity is present at the L4 vertebral body, appearing to involve the superior and anterior aspects predominantly. The degree of uptake at both sites is substantially above background and is disproportionate to degenerative change. SPECT/CT correlation confirms that these foci correspond to the vertebral bodies rather than the posterior elements, making degenerative facet disease an unlikely explanation. The findings are highly suspicious for skeletal metastatic involvement at L3 and L4.

Sacrum and Iliac Bones:
Focal, moderately increased tracer activity is seen at the right iliac crest, corresponding to a discrete lesion on SPECT/CT without associated cortical disruption on the low-dose CT component. An additional focus of mildly to moderately elevated uptake is present at the right sacral ala, lateral to the sacral foramina, which is asymmetric relative to the contralateral side and is not accounted for by sacroiliac joint degenerative changes. Both foci are felt to represent additional osseous metastatic deposits.

Left Iliac Bone and Acetabulum:
No convincing focal uptake abnormality. Mild, symmetric periarticular uptake at the left hip joint in keeping with degenerative change.

Proximal Femora:
No focal lesions. Uptake at the femoral heads is symmetric and physiologic.

Distal Lower Extremities:
No suspicious foci identified bilaterally through the knees, tibiae, fibulae, or feet. Vascular phase uptake is symmetric.

Ribs and Sternum:
No clearly abnormal foci. A small area of mildly increased uptake at the anterior left 8th rib is noted, which is not definitively elevated above adjacent ribs and may represent a prior traumatic rib fracture; clinical correlation is recommended, though metastatic involvement cannot be excluded at this site. This finding is of low suspicion and was not counted as a definitive lesion.

Shoulder Girdle and Upper Extremities:
No abnormal tracer accumulation. Uptake at the acromioclavicular joints bilaterally is symmetric and consistent with degenerative arthropathy.

Kidneys and Bladder:
Normal renal excretion of tracer with symmetric renal uptake. Bladder is well-visualized with expected activity. No evidence of the diffusely supersaturated skeletal uptake with faint renal visualization ("superscan" pattern) that would suggest extremely heavy tumor burden replacing normal marrow.

Soft Tissues:
No unexpected soft tissue tracer retention.

IMPRESSION — BONE SCAN:
1. Multifocal osseous abnormalities consistent with skeletal metastatic disease: definitive involvement at L3 vertebral body, L4 vertebral body, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala. Four sites of involvement identified.
2. No evidence of a superscan pattern.
3. Indeterminate uptake at anterior left 8th rib — low suspicion, likely post-traumatic but cannot completely exclude metastatic involvement; correlate with CT and clinical history.
4. No new axial or appendicular skeletal lesions beyond those described.

------------------------------------------------------------
EXAMINATION 2: CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST
------------------------------------------------------------

TECHNIQUE:
Multi-detector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of approximately 120 mL of iohexol (Omnipaque 350) intravenously via right antecubital access using a standard portal venous phase protocol. Coronal and sagittal reformations were generated. Oral contrast was not administered. Images reviewed on PACS workstation in standard soft-tissue, bone, and lung windows.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The lungs are well-aerated bilaterally. No parenchymal nodule, consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural-based lesion is identified. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No endobronchial lesion. No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The mediastinal contour is within normal limits for patient age. Trachea is midline. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes by size criteria. The superior mediastinal structures are symmetric. No hilar lymphadenopathy. The thymus is involuted as expected.

Heart and Pericardium:
Cardiac silhouette is at the upper limits of normal in transverse diameter. No pericardial effusion. Coronary artery calcification is present at the left anterior descending and circumflex arteries, consistent with atherosclerotic disease; this is a non-emergent, incidental finding. Aortic valve appears tricuspid with no significant calcification. No ascending aortic aneurysm.

Thoracic Aorta and Vasculature:
The thoracic aorta demonstrates atherosclerotic mural calcification without aneurysmal dilatation. No dissection flap identified. The pulmonary arteries opacify homogeneously; no filling defect to suggest pulmonary embolism within the main, lobar, or segmental pulmonary arteries.

Bone Windows — Thorax:
Sclerotic foci at T11 and scattered other thoracic vertebrae are noted; these are largely consistent with degenerative endplate changes or benign bone islands and appear too small and non-specific to characterize definitively. No lytic destructive lesion or pathologic cortical breakthrough is seen in the thoracic cage.

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and contour. Hepatic parenchyma is homogeneous in attenuation without focal lesion, enhancing mass, or cystic abnormality. No biliary ductal dilatation. The portal and hepatic veins are patent.

Spleen:
No splenomegaly. The spleen is homogeneous without focal lesion or splenic vein thrombosis.

Pancreas:
The pancreas is normal in size and contour with no focal parenchymal abnormality, ductal dilatation, or peripancreatic inflammatory change.

Adrenal Glands:
Both adrenal glands are normal in morphology and size. No adrenal mass or abnormal enhancement to suggest metastatic involvement.

Kidneys and Ureters:
The kidneys are symmetric in size and enhance uniformly in the cortical and medullary phases. No hydronephrosis or hydroureter. No renal mass or calculus. The renal pelves are non-dilated.

Retroperitoneal and Mesenteric Lymph Nodes:
No pathologically enlarged para-aortic, paracaval, or mesenteric lymph nodes. Several small sub-centimeter retroperitoneal lymph nodes are present but do not reach a threshold for pathologic enlargement.

Bowel:
Visualized small bowel and colon are unremarkable without wall thickening, obstruction, or pneumatosis. No free intraperitoneal air. No ascites.

PELVIS:

Prostate Gland:
The prostate gland is significantly enlarged, measuring approximately 4.8 cm in greatest transverse dimension. The normal zonal anatomy is disrupted. There is loss of the well-defined capsular fat plane posterolaterally bilaterally, most pronounced on the right, with apparent extension of soft tissue beyond the prostatic capsule — findings consistent with extracapsular extension. The seminal vesicles appear asymmetric; the right seminal vesicle demonstrates soft tissue thickening and loss of the normal fat plane between it and the prostate gland base, raising concern for right seminal vesicle involvement, though correlation with the clinical and pathologic findings is recommended. The left seminal vesicle is less clearly involved.

Lymph Nodes — Pelvis:
A cluster of lymph nodes in the right external iliac chain is clearly enlarged. The dominant right external iliac node measures approximately 1.8 cm in short-axis diameter. Several immediately adjacent smaller right external iliac nodes also exceed the 1.0 cm short-axis threshold. No left-sided iliac or obturator lymphadenopathy meeting size criteria, though a few small left external iliac nodes at the margin of normal are present. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Bladder:
The bladder is adequately distended and the wall is smooth without focal mass or thickening. No intraluminal filling defect. No evidence of direct prostatic invasion into the bladder base on these images, though the interface is intimate.

Rectum:
No rectal wall thickening or perirectal adenopathy.

Bony Pelvis — CT Detail:
There is heterogeneous osseous density in the right iliac wing, corresponding to the area of increased uptake on the bone scan, demonstrating ill-defined sclerosis without frank cortical destruction. A corresponding area of ill-defined sclerosis is noted at the right sacral ala. These osseous changes are consistent with blastic/mixed metastatic deposits in the appropriate clinical context.

Soft Tissues:
No pelvic free fluid. No soft tissue mass outside the prostate and nodal regions described.

------------------------------------------------------------
OVERALL IMPRESSION
------------------------------------------------------------

1. PROSTATE GLAND: Markedly enlarged prostate (approximately 4.8 cm) with imaging findings highly suspicious for extracapsular extension and probable right seminal vesicle involvement, consistent with at least cT3b disease. This correlates with the clinical staging.

2. REGIONAL LYMPH NODE METASTASES: Enlarged right external iliac lymph nodes (dominant node 1.8 cm short-axis), consistent with pelvic regional nodal metastatic disease (cN1).

3. OSSEOUS METASTATIC DISEASE: Multiple skeletal deposits at L3 and L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala demonstrated on both bone scintigraphy and CT, consistent with blastic/mixed osseous metastases (cM1b). Four definitive sites of skeletal involvement identified. No superscan.

4. NO VISCERAL METASTASES: The liver, lungs, adrenal glands, and other visceral organs are free of metastatic involvement on this examination.

5. INCIDENTAL FINDINGS: Coronary artery atherosclerotic calcification (clinical correlation for cardiovascular risk management); indeterminate anterior left 8th rib uptake on bone scan (low suspicion, likely benign).

In summary, imaging findings are consistent with newly diagnosed, high-volume metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma with regional pelvic lymph node and skeletal metastases, without visceral disease. These findings support a stage IVB clinical classification.

Electronic signature: [Radiologist, MD — Diagnostic Radiology / Nuclear Medicine]
